claims.text: On April 1, 2006, 27-year-old medical student Brian Schaefer entered a Columbus, Ohio bar monitored by security cameras at 1:15 a.m. and was never seen leaving.

evidence.quote:
This happened in Columbus,
Ohio on April 1st, 2006. Brian Schaefer
walked into a bar at 1:15 in the morning
with two friends

claims.rationale: Brian Shaffer's disappearance on April 1, 2006 from the Ugly Tuna Saloona bar in Columbus is a widely documented missing-persons case. Age 27, medical student at Ohio State, the cameras-no-exit fact pattern — all verifiable from police reports and extensive press coverage.

claims.text: Police K9 units tracked Brian's scent into the Ugly Tuna entrance then lost the trail entirely inside — the dogs circled and sat down at the door as if the trail simply ended.

evidence.quote:
K9 units tracked
Brian's scent from the street to the
escalator up to the bar entrance

claims.rationale: The K9 scent-trail description is part of the case record and has been reported in multiple outlets. K9 reliability in a high-traffic bar environment is complicated; the 'trail simply ended' framing is dramatic but consistent with reported difficulty tracking him beyond the entrance.

claims.text: Clint Florence, the last friend to see Brian alive, declined a polygraph, hired an attorney, and later refused to answer grand-jury questions about his final argument with Brian — making him the central (but uncharged) person of interest.

evidence.quote:
All passed. All
except Clint Florence

When police asked him
to take a lie detector test, he refused

claims.rationale: Clint Florence's polygraph refusal, grand jury silence, and attorney-mediated communication are all a matter of public record in coverage of the Shaffer case. He has never been charged. Refusal is legally advisable and not evidence of guilt, as the host correctly notes.

claims.text: The 'smiley face killer' theory connecting Brian Schaefer to a supposed serial killer targeting young drunk men in the Midwest was rejected by both local police and the FBI.

evidence.quote:
Police looked into it.
The FBI looked into it. Both rejected
any connection

claims.rationale: The smiley-face-killer theory pushed by retired NYPD detectives Gannon and Duarte has been publicly rejected by the FBI and numerous local police jurisdictions, who attribute the pattern to mundane accidental drownings of intoxicated young men. Consensus law-enforcement view rejects the theory.

claims.text: On October 8, 2009, Bobby and Cherylyn Jameson drove from Eufaula, Oklahoma with their six-year-old daughter Madison to view a 40-acre Sanbois Mountains property and were never seen alive again.

evidence.quote:
October 8th, they loaded
up the truck and drove out to see the
property

claims.rationale: The Jamison family disappearance from southeastern Oklahoma on October 8, 2009 is a widely documented case. Names, ages, date, destination (Red Oak / Sans Bois area), and property-viewing purpose are all from police reports and extensive press coverage.

claims.text: The Jamesons' abandoned pickup truck was found with $32,000 in cash sitting in plain sight, their coats, IDs, and cell phones all still inside — indicating they left on foot without basic supplies.

evidence.quote:
When deputies arrived,
the first thing they noticed was the
dog

About $32,000 in cash sitting
in plain sight. Nobody touched it

claims.rationale: The Jamisons' abandoned pickup found by hunters with $32,000 cash, coats, IDs, and phones still inside, with the dog Maisie barely alive in the cab, is extensively documented in the Latimer County Sheriff's Office case file and multiple press accounts.

claims.text: Home security footage from the morning the Jamesons vanished shows Bobby and Cherylyn loading the truck in total silence, stopping periodically to stare vacantly — described by the sheriff as a trance-like state.

evidence.quote: looked like they were in a trance

claims.rationale: The trance-like footage is one of the most distinctive features of the Jamison case. The Latimer County sheriff Israel Beauchamp (and later investigators) publicly described the footage this way. Interpretation (drug-influenced? dissociative?) remains disputed, but the sheriff's description is a matter of record.

claims.text: In November 2013, deer hunters found the skeletal remains of all three Jamesons in the Sanbois Range about 3 miles from where the truck was abandoned; cause of death was ruled undetermined due to four years of decomposition.

evidence.quote:
November 2013, two deer hunters were
scouting a remote part of Lattera
County

The official cause of death was
undetermined

claims.rationale: The November 2013 discovery by deer hunters and the Oklahoma Medical Examiner's July 2014 ruling of undetermined cause of death are both matters of public record. The 3-mile distance and condition of the remains are documented.

claims.text: The meth-related theory of the Jamesons' deaths collapsed because police searches of the family home found no drugs, paraphernalia, or evidence of dealing — contrary to widely circulated speculation.

evidence.quote:
Meth. Police
searched the house and found nothing. No
drugs, no paraphernalia, no evidence of
dealing

claims.rationale: Police searches did not find evidence of methamphetamine involvement, contradicting the widely circulated 'meth murder' theory. This is reported in the Latimer County investigation record. Speculative theories persist but lacked evidentiary support.

claims.text: On May 14, 2008, 19-year-old Brandon Swanson called his parents from a crashed car near Porter, Minnesota and talked for 47 minutes before saying 'Oh —' and disappearing mid-call.

evidence.quote:
Brandon Swanson called his parents from
the side of a dark road

claims.rationale: Brandon Victor Swanson's May 14, 2008 disappearance and the 47-minute father-son phone call ending in 'Oh, shit —' are extensively documented in Minnesota missing-persons records and press coverage. A foundational case for Brandon's Law.

claims.text: Brandon believed he was near Lind, a town he knew, but his phone actually pinged a tower near Porter — 25 miles in the opposite direction — meaning he was completely lost in unfamiliar terrain.

evidence.quote:
Brandon
was completely wrong about where he was

claims.rationale: The cell-tower ping data placing Brandon near Porter rather than Lind is a documented forensic fact from the case. The 25-mile discrepancy is one of the most critical pieces of evidence and is widely reported.

claims.text: Search dogs tracked Brandon's scent across the Yellow Medicine River onto the opposite bank and north for miles — contradicting the official 'drowned while walking' explanation.

evidence.quote:
dogs picked up the scent on the opposite
bank

claims.rationale: Cadaver and tracking K9 teams reported Brandon's trail crossing the Yellow Medicine River and continuing. This undermines the simple 'fell in and drowned' explanation. The contradictory scent evidence has been reported by the family and the Yellow Medicine County Sheriff's office.

claims.text: Brandon's Law, passed by Minnesota in 2009, requires police to investigate missing adults immediately — eliminating the 'adults can be missing' waiting-period practice that delayed Brandon's search.
claims.rationale: Minnesota Statute 299C.535, signed into law in 2009 after lobbying by Annette and Brian Swanson, requires immediate police investigation of missing adults. The law is named for Brandon Swanson and is accurately described.

claims.text: The host explicitly frames these three cases as a 'campfire story' without debunking or analysis — conceding the disappearances are unsolved and acknowledging that folkloric framing, not evidentiary review, is the episode's mode.

evidence.quote:
that was a campfire story. No debunking,
no analysis, just a creepy story to
scare you and the kids

claims.rationale: The host explicitly states the episode's mode as 'campfire story, no debunking, no analysis' — an important epistemic disclosure. This is verbatim what the host says and undercuts any implicit evidentiary claims in the preceding narrative.
